name: Data Modeling
description: Declarative, immutable data models with type-safe constraints that compose naturally

content.title: Data Modeling
content.content:
# Data Modeling

The secret to maintainable data models is not elaborate ORMs or rigid schemas - it's **immutable records with declarative constraints** that compose naturally and evolve safely. Traditional data modeling struggles with mutable state bugs, complex inheritance hierarchies, tight database coupling, and migration nightmares. Teams spend more time managing entity frameworks and preventing race conditions than modeling actual business domains.

MeshWeaver's data modeling takes a different approach: **immutable C# records** as domain entities, **data annotations** for declarative validation, and **string-based references** for loose coupling. Build once, compose everywhere. Change your data model, and immutability prevents entire categories of bugs. No race conditions to debug, no migration scripts for nullable columns, no inheritance complexity that breaks when requirements change.

## Architecture: Entities, Relationships, and Change Tracking

MeshWeaver separates data modeling concerns into distinct layers that work together:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Domain Entity - Person] -->|String ID Reference| B[Related Entity - Todo]
    B --> C[Enum State Machine]
    C --> D[Status: Pending/InProgress/Completed]
    A --> E[EntityStore - Collections]
    B --> E
    E --> F[Change Tracking - EntityUpdate]
    F --> G[Immutable History]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#ffe1f5
    style D fill:#e1ffe1
    style E fill:#f5e1ff
    style F fill:#ffe1e1
```

**Entities** are immutable records that define business concepts - what data exists and how it's constrained. **Relationships** use string-based ID references for loose coupling. **State machines** use enums to define valid states and transitions. **Collections** use ImmutableDictionary for thread-safe storage. When data changes, EntityStore creates EntityUpdate records, providing full audit trails automatically.

## Core Data Modeling Concepts

| Concept | Purpose | Implementation |
|---------|---------|----------------|
| **Entities** | Domain objects representing business concepts | Immutable C# records with init-only properties |
| **Attributes** | Data fields with constraints | Data annotations ([Key], [Required]) for declarative validation |
| **Relationships** | Associations between entities | String-based ID references (loose coupling) |
| **State Machines** | Lifecycle and status tracking | Enums with well-defined transitions |
| **Collections** | Grouped entities with indexing | ImmutableDictionary for thread-safe access |

## Example 1: Person - Simple Domain Entity

Consider a claims system tracking adjusters and policyholders. Traditional approaches require writing setters, validation logic, null checks, and careful threading - dozens of lines of defensive code.

With MeshWeaver:

```csharp
public record Person
{
    [Key]
    public string Id { get; init; } = string.Empty;

    [Required]
    public string Name { get; init; } = string.Empty;

    public string? Email { get; init; }

    public DateTimeOffset CreatedAt { get; init; } = DateTimeOffset.UtcNow;
}
```

**What happens**: The `record` keyword makes Person immutable by design. The `[Key]` attribute identifies the primary key. `[Required]` provides declarative validation enforced automatically. `init` setters allow construction but prevent mutation after creation. Default values (`string.Empty`, `DateTimeOffset.UtcNow`) initialize required properties. Nullable types (`string?`) explicitly mark optional fields.

**Benefits**: No setters means no state bugs. No null reference exceptions with proper nullable annotations. Immutability enables safe concurrent access without locks. Data annotations drive framework validation automatically. Updates use `with` expressions that create new records, leaving originals unchanged.

## Example 2: Todo - Aggregate with Relationships and State

Tracking claims requires managing assignments, priorities, and status transitions. Traditional approaches need complex state machines, foreign key management, and careful lifecycle handling.

With MeshWeaver:

```csharp
public record Todo
{
    [Key]
    public string Id { get; init; } = string.Empty;

    [Required]
    public string Title { get; init; } = string.Empty;

    public string? Description { get; init; }

    public TaskPriority Priority { get; init; } = TaskPriority.Medium;

    public TodoStatus Status { get; init; } = TodoStatus.Pending;

    public string? Assignee { get; init; }  // Reference to Person.Id

    public DateTimeOffset CreatedAt { get; init; } = DateTimeOffset.UtcNow;

    public DateTimeOffset? CompletedAt { get; init; }
}

public enum TaskPriority { Low, Medium, High, Urgent }

public enum TodoStatus { Pending, InProgress, Completed, Cancelled }
```

**What happens**: TodoStatus enum defines all valid states at compile time. The Assignee property uses a string reference to Person.Id, avoiding tight coupling. Priority defaults to Medium, Status to Pending - sensible business defaults. CreatedAt always has a value. CompletedAt stays null until the task completes, enabling temporal queries.

**Benefits**: Enum state machines prevent invalid states - impossible to have Status="WhateverTypo". String references enable flexible relationships without circular dependencies or lazy loading complexity. Temporal fields (CreatedAt, CompletedAt) enable audit trails and analytics queries. Immutability means status transitions require creating new records with `with` expressions, making state changes explicit and auditable.

## Example 3: EntityStore - Collection Patterns with Change Tracking

Managing collections of entities across threads requires careful synchronization. Traditional approaches need locks, concurrent collections, and manual change tracking.

With MeshWeaver:

```csharp
public record EntityStore
{
    public ImmutableDictionary<string, InstanceCollection> Collections { get; init; }
        = ImmutableDictionary<string, InstanceCollection>.Empty;

    internal IEnumerable<EntityUpdate> ComputeChanges(
        string collection,
        InstanceCollection updated)
    {
        var existing = Collections.GetValueOrDefault(collection);
        if (existing == null)
            return updated.Instances.Values
                .Select(instance => new EntityUpdate(
                    collection,
                    instance.Id,
                    ChangeType.Added,
                    instance));

        // Compute adds, updates, deletes...
    }
}

public record EntityUpdate(
    string Collection,
    string Id,
    ChangeType Type,
    object Instance
);

public enum ChangeType { Added, Updated, Deleted }
```

**What happens**: EntityStore uses ImmutableDictionary for thread-safe collections. ComputeChanges compares old and new collections, returning EntityUpdate records describing every change. The ChangeType enum defines all mutation types. EntityUpdate records are immutable, serializable, and can be stored for audit trails.

**Benefits**: ImmutableDictionary eliminates race conditions without locks. Change tracking provides automatic audit history - every add, update, and delete captured as a record. Record types make change events serializable for persistence or streaming. Thread-safe by design - multiple threads can read simultaneously, updates create new dictionaries.

## Immutability in Action

All three examples share the same immutability pattern:

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Application
    participant Store as EntityStore
    participant Person as Person Entity
    participant Todo as Todo Entity

    App->>Store: Add Person(Id="P1", Name="Alice")
    Store->>Store: Create EntityUpdate(Added)
    Store->>Person: Store immutable record

    App->>Store: Add Todo(Assignee="P1")
    Store->>Store: Create EntityUpdate(Added)
    Store->>Todo: Store with string reference to P1

    App->>Todo: Update Status to InProgress
    Note over Todo: Original record unchanged
    Todo->>Store: Create new Todo with Status=InProgress
    Store->>Store: Create EntityUpdate(Updated)

    App->>Store: Query todos by Assignee="P1"
    Store->>App: Return todos (string reference enables flexible queries)
```

The application never mutates records. It creates new records with changes. EntityStore tracks every change. String references enable flexible queries without loading entire object graphs.

## Best Practices

**Declarative Constraints**: Use data annotations rather than imperative validation.

```csharp
// Good - Declarative
[Required, StringLength(100)]
public string Name { get; init; }

// Avoid - Imperative
public string Name
{
    get => _name;
    init => _name = value?.Length <= 100 
        ? value 
        : throw new Exception();
}
```

**Immutability Patterns**: Use `record` types with `init` setters, never mutable properties.

```csharp
// Good - Immutable
public record Person { public string Name { get; init; } }
var updated = person with { Name = "New Name" };

// Avoid - Mutable
public class Person { public string Name { get; set; } }
person.Name = "New Name";  // Mutates original
```

**Relationship Modeling**: Use string IDs for loose coupling, not object references.

```csharp
// Good - String reference
public string? Assignee { get; init; }  // Reference to Person.Id

// Avoid - Direct reference
public Person? Assignee { get; init; }  // Tight coupling, serialization issues
```

**Enum State Machines**: Define all valid states explicitly.

```csharp
public TodoStatus? NextStatus(TodoStatus current) => current switch
{
    TodoStatus.Pending => TodoStatus.InProgress,
    TodoStatus.InProgress => TodoStatus.Completed,
    TodoStatus.Completed => null,  // Terminal state
    _ => throw new InvalidOperationException($"Unknown: {current}")
};
```

**Temporal Data**: Use nullable DateTimeOffset for event timestamps.

```csharp
public DateTimeOffset CreatedAt { get; init; } = DateTimeOffset.UtcNow;
public DateTimeOffset? CompletedAt { get; init; }  // Null until completed
```

**Type Safety**: Leverage nullable reference types and required properties.

```csharp
#nullable enable
public record Person
{
    [Required]
    public string Name { get; init; } = string.Empty;  // Non-nullable

    public string? Email { get; init; }  // Explicitly nullable
}
```

## Traditional vs MeshWeaver Data Modeling

| Aspect | Traditional Data Modeling | MeshWeaver Data Modeling |
|--------|---------------------------|------------------------|
| **Mutability** | Mutable classes with setters, state bugs common | Immutable records with init setters, state bugs impossible |
| **Relationships** | Object references, tight coupling, circular dependencies | String-based ID references, loose coupling, flexible queries |
| **Validation** | Imperative validation in setters or separate validators | Declarative data annotations, automatically enforced |
| **State Management** | Complex state machines with many guards | Enum-driven states with pattern matching |
| **Collections** | Mutable collections requiring locks | ImmutableDictionary for thread-safe access |
| **Change Tracking** | Manual audit logs, often inconsistent | Automatic EntityUpdate records for every change |

## Advanced Patterns

**Cardinality Patterns**: Model one-to-one, one-to-many, and many-to-many relationships with string references.

- **One-to-One**: `public string? SpouseId { get; init; }`
- **One-to-Many**: `public string ParentId { get; init; }` in child entity
- **Many-to-Many**: Junction entity with two string ID properties

**Normalization in MeshWeaver**: Entities are naturally normalized. No embedded objects by default. String references ensure referential integrity at query time. Immutability prevents update anomalies.

**Composition with `with` expressions**: Update immutable records declaratively.

```csharp
var updatedTodo = todo with
{
    Status = TodoStatus.Completed,
    CompletedAt = DateTimeOffset.UtcNow
};
```

**Default Values and Required Fields**: Combine data annotations with sensible defaults.

```csharp
[Required]
public string Id { get; init; } = Guid.NewGuid().ToString();
```

## The Result: Safe Evolution

MeshWeaver's data modeling approach turns traditional database design challenges into a composition exercise. Build immutable records with declarative constraints. Model relationships with string references. Track state with enums. The framework manages complexity - serialization, validation, change tracking, thread safety - while you focus on domain logic. When requirements change, immutability ensures safe evolution. No migration scripts for state bugs that can't happen. No race conditions in collections that can't mutate. Complex, evolving data models that stay maintainable as complexity grows, because the architecture scales with composition rather than fighting it.
